Note Tail Documentation

Overview

The Note Tail container adds a smooth cosine fade-out at the end of a note, preventing click artifacts and enabling let-ring sustain effects. When a note ends, the audio engine enters tail mode — rather than cutting the signal abruptly, the Note Tail container allows the upstream signal to continue oscillating while applying a cosine envelope that fades it to silence over a configurable duration.

Without a Note Tail, short or abruptly-ended notes can produce audible clicks at the cut-off point. With Note Tail, even a note with zero written duration will fade gracefully, and long tail lengths create a harp-like "let ring" effect where the sound continues beyond the written note length.

Category

Essential (Blue) - Core sound generation

Ports

Input Ports

Port Data Type Default Description
signalIn Signal 0.0 The audio signal to fade out at note end
length Control — Modulates the tail length (in ms) from an upstream control source. Applied on top of the tailLength parameter via the connection function.

Output Ports

Port Data Type Description
signalOut Signal The faded signal: passthrough during the note, cosine-faded during tail

Parameters

Parameter Type Default Range Description
tailLength Float 200.0 1–9999 Duration of the cosine fade-out in milliseconds

How It Works

During the Note

While a note is playing, Note Tail is a transparent passthrough — signalIn passes to signalOut unchanged. There is no processing overhead or coloration during normal playback.

At Note End (Tail Mode)

When the note ends, the audio engine enters tail mode. With a Note Tail container present, the upstream signal sources (Harmonic Generator, Wavetable Synth, etc.) keep running rather than being silenced. Note Tail receives the live oscillating signal and applies a cosine fade envelope:

progress   = 1.0 − (samplesRemaining / totalSamples)
fadeFactor = 0.5 × (1.0 + cos(π × progress))

signalOut = signalIn × fadeFactor

The cosine shape is smooth at both ends — it starts at 1.0 (full volume), stays relatively flat early on, then curves gracefully to 0.0. This avoids the linear-fade audible "knee" and produces a natural, musical decay.

progress fadeFactor Effect
0.0 (start of tail) 1.0 Full volume — seamless continuation
0.25 ≈ 0.85 Subtle reduction
0.5 0.5 Half amplitude (–6 dB)
0.75 ≈ 0.15 Near silence
1.0 (end of tail) 0.0 Complete silence

After the tail completes, the audio engine stops rendering tail samples and the note is fully done.

Tail Length and "Let Ring"

The tailLength parameter (1–9999 ms) controls the total duration of the cosine fade. Unlike a simple gate or mute, the sound keeps oscillating during the entire fade — so long tail lengths produce an authentic sustain effect, not a faded silence. This is musically equivalent to a harp or piano "let ring" marking.

  • Short tail (10–50 ms): Click suppression only — just smooth the note cutoff
  • Medium tail (100–500 ms): Natural instrument ring-out
  • Long tail (1000–5000 ms): Pronounced sustain, sound continues well past the written note
  • Maximum tail (9999 ms): Nearly 10-second sustain — full "let ring" harp effect

Length Port Modulation

The length input port allows the tail duration to be controlled by an upstream modulator. This is especially useful with Frequency Mapper to make lower-pitched notes sustain longer than high-pitched ones (mimicking acoustic instrument behaviour):

[Frequency Mapper] → controlOut → length
  outputMin: 500    (long tail at low pitch)
  outputMax: 80     (short tail at high pitch)
  curveType: Logarithmic
             [Note Tail]
              tailLength: 200    (base value, modulated by connection)

The length connection is applied using the connection's function and weight, then clamped to 1–9999 ms before being passed to the fade engine.

Behaviour with Physical Models

Recorder, Bowed, and Reed containers have their own internal tail behaviour — they use special tail-mode rendering that lets their physical models decay naturally. Note Tail does not apply its cosine fade to these sources; instead, each physical model manages its own ring-out independently. This means:

  • Harmonic Generator, Wavetable Synth, Noise Color Filter: Note Tail cosine fade works directly on the live signal
  • Recorder, Bowed, Reed: Use their own internal decay — Note Tail has no additional effect on these sources
  • IR Convolution: Always drains its impulse response tail regardless of Note Tail

Placement in the Signal Graph

Note Tail should be placed after all signal processing and before the final output. It replaces the output container in a typical graph:

[Harmonic Generator] → [Rolloff Processor] → [Spectrum to Signal]
                                                      ↓
                                               [Note Tail] → signalOut (final output)

Only one Note Tail container is needed per sounit graph. Multiple Note Tail containers are not useful — only the last one in the execution order affects the final output.

Usage Patterns

Click Suppression Only

[any signal chain] → signalIn → [Note Tail] → signalOut
  tailLength: 20     (20 ms — inaudible as sustain, removes click)

Natural Ring-Out

[Harmonic Generator] → [Spectrum to Signal] → signalIn
                                                    ↓
                                             [Note Tail] → signalOut
                                              tailLength: 400

Pitch-Dependent Tail (Frequency Mapper)

[Frequency Mapper] → controlOut → length
  outputMin: 2000    (2 seconds for low notes)
  outputMax: 100     (100 ms for high notes)
  curveType: Logarithmic
             [Note Tail]
              tailLength: 500    (base, overridden by connection)

This approach mimics how real acoustic instruments sustain longer at lower pitches (longer string/resonator decay) and shorter at higher pitches.

Let-Ring Guitar

[Karplus Strong] → signalIn → [Note Tail] → signalOut
  KS: mode = 0 (String), damping = 0.2
  Note Tail: tailLength = 3000   (3-second "let ring")

Troubleshooting

No Fade — Sound Cuts Abruptly

  1. Confirm Note Tail's signalIn is connected to the upstream signal chain
  2. Confirm Note Tail is the final output container (its signalOut is the graph output)
  3. Verify tailLength is greater than 1 ms

Tail Seems Too Short Regardless of Setting

  1. Check that the Note Tail container is in the correct sounit — a saved sounit is loaded as a variation and uses its own graph copy
  2. If using a Frequency Mapper on the length port, ensure its output range is set correctly and not overriding to a small value

Sound During Tail Is Distorted or Wrong Pitch

The upstream source keeps running during tail mode. If the source uses pitch tracking (e.g., it is pitch-modulated), the tail continues at whatever pitch the note ended at. This is expected behaviour.

Recorder / Bowed / Reed Not Affected by Note Tail

Physical models manage their own internal tail decay and are not routed through Note Tail's cosine fade. Their sustain and decay behaviour is controlled by their own parameters (e.g., Recorder's decay, Bowed's bow pressure).

Documentation accurate as of Kala v1.4. Source files: tailprocessor.h, tailprocessor.cpp
